18cu|- When expanding researcher notes, stay faithful to their claims and emphasis. You may tighten, clarify, and organize — but do not introduce new arguments or substantive claims the researcher did not make.
19la|- Flag any place where the text goes beyond what the researcher supplied: use `[inference]` or ask for confirmation.
20kg|- When the researcher says "write a literature review on X," the first step is to ask what sources they have or want covered, not to fabricate citations.
21st|
22et|---
23rd|
24fq|## 2. Thesis Structure Guide
25th|
26ni|### Chapter Organization
27th|
28gf|| Chapter | Purpose | Typical Length (pages) | Notes |
29yf||---|---|---|---|
30bu|| Introduction | Establish research gap, state problem, pose questions, outline thesis | 10–20 | Funnel structure (see §8) |
31dk|| Literature Review | Map the field, identify gaps, position the study | 25–40 | Thematic or chronological; must end with a gap statement |
32ma|| Methodology | Justify and describe research design, data, and analysis | 15–30 | Must defend choices, not just describe them |
33zo|| Results | Present findings without interpretation | 20–40 | Tables, figures, descriptive statistics; raw reporting |
34pb|| Discussion | Interpret results, connect to literature, acknowledge limits | 15–30 | See discussion structure in §8 |
35dq|| Conclusion | Summarize contributions, state implications, suggest future work | 5–10 | No new evidence |
36th|
37eq|### Introduction Checklist
38sp|- [ ] Broad context: why does this area matter?
39kt|- [ ] Specific problem: what gap, contradiction, or unanswered question exists?
40za|- [ ] Research questions or hypotheses stated explicitly
41tc|- [ ] Contribution statement: what this thesis does that prior work does not
42hg|- [ ] Thesis outline (roadmap of chapters)
43rd|
44ez|### Literature Review Principles
45dv|- Organize thematically, not as an annotated list
46bu|- Every source cited must serve the argument (establish consensus, show gaps, contrast positions)
47zu|- End with an explicit gap statement: "Despite X, Y remains unaddressed…"
48am|- Group sources by idea, not by author; avoid the "Smith (2020) found… Jones (2021) found…" pattern without synthesis
49th|
50zw|### Methodology Must Include
51jy|- Research paradigm and rationale (why this approach, not just what)
52cw|- Data collection: sources, sampling, instruments, procedures
53tl|- Analysis method and justification
54di|- Validity/reliability/trustworthiness measures
55sz|- Ethical considerations
56nh|- Limitations acknowledged up front
57th|
58yd|---
59th|
60ls|## 3. Paper Structure Templates
61st|
62vl|### 3a. IMRAD (Empirical / Experimental)
63rd|
64if|```
65qr|Abstract (150–250 words, structured or unstructured)
66jj|1. Introduction
67wb|   - Context and significance
68vr|   - Literature positioning
69vf|   - Research question / hypothesis
70rb|   - Paper organization
71ni|2. Methods
72mk|   - Participants / Materials / Procedure
73er|   - Analytical approach
74qr|   - Justification for each choice
75uw|3. Results
76vf|   - Findings organized by research question
77bj|   - Tables and figures with descriptive captions
78nj|   - No interpretation here
79hb|4. Discussion
80kk|   - Interpret each finding
81uv|   - Connect to prior literature
82qw|   - Limitations
83vj|   - Implications and future directions
84qu|5. Conclusion (sometimes merged with Discussion)
85wg|References
86dv|Appendices (if any)
87ri|```
88th|
89am|### 3b. Argument-Driven (Theoretical / Analytical)
90th|
91eh|```
92jq|Abstract (150–250 words)
93jj|1. Introduction
94zx|   - Problem statement
95xc|   - Why existing frameworks fall short
96je|   - Thesis statement (the argument)
97gy|2. Conceptual / Theoretical Grounding
98ft|   - Key concepts defined
99mh|   - Prior frameworks analyzed
100rf|   - Gaps or tensions identified
101dk|3. Analysis (may be multiple sections)
102yl|   - Each section advances one sub-argument
103sd|   - Evidence: textual, logical, case-based, comparative
104hb|4. Discussion
105on|   - Synthesize sub-arguments into main claim
106ra|   - Respond to anticipated objections
107qw|   - Limitations
108uh|5. Conclusion
109fh|   - Restate contribution
110qt|   - Broader implications
111wg|References
112lb|```
113th|
114fj|### 3c. Systematic Review
115th|
116wd|```
117vi|Abstract (structured: Background, Objectives, Methods, Results, Conclusions)
118jj|1. Introduction
119rh|   - Rationale for the review
120va|   - Review questions
121ni|2. Methods
122nh|   - Search strategy (databases, terms, date range)
123kv|   - Inclusion/exclusion criteria
124sk|   - Screening process (PRISMA flow)
125jp|   - Data extraction and synthesis method
126bw|   - Quality appraisal
127uw|3. Results
128jc|   - Search outcomes
129rv|   - Study characteristics (summary table)
130xa|   - Synthesis by theme/question
131hr|   - Quality assessment results
132hb|4. Discussion
133ap|   - Summary of findings
134sz|   - Comparison with prior reviews
135ue|   - Strengths and limitations of the review
136ur|   - Implications for practice and research
137uh|5. Conclusion
138wg|References
139ni|Appendices (search strings, full extraction tables)
140wd|```
141st|
142zf|---
143rd|
144ln|## 4. Academic Register Principles
145th|
146kk|### Precision
147qa|- Use the most specific term available: "three-item scale" not "a brief measure"; "statistically significant at α = .01" not "highly significant"
148dn|- Define technical terms on first use; do not assume disciplinary consensus on meaning
149th|
150er|### Hedging (Calibrated by Claim Strength)
151st|
152wo|| Claim Strength | Typical Hedge | Example |
153mh||---|---|---|
154ib|| Strong (direct evidence) | indicates, demonstrates | "The results indicate that…" |
155nm|| Moderate (correlational) | suggests, is associated with | "This pattern suggests a relationship between…" |
156wx|| Weak (speculative) | may, could, might, appears to | "These findings may reflect…" |
157gn|| Very weak (exploratory) | it is possible that, one interpretation is | "One possible interpretation is that…" |
158th|
159ga|**Rules:**
160dh|- Never hedge a direct replication: if you replicated a known result, say so directly.
161tt|- Never claim more than the data supports: "proves" is almost always wrong; "establishes" only for deductive arguments.
162rp|- Avoid hedge stacking: "It might perhaps possibly suggest…" — pick one calibrated term.
163rd|
164uh|### Formal but Not Stiff
165kv|- Prefer "we argue" over "it is argued by the authors"
166xp|- Prefer "the data show" over "it can be seen that the data show"
167pp|- Contractions are genre-dependent: avoid in formal journal articles; acceptable in some conference papers and most theses after supervisor approval
168sj|- Avoid ornamentalLatin: "e.g." and "i.e." are fine; "ibid.," "op. cit.," and "viz." add friction without clarity
169th|
170az|### Discipline-Specific Conventions
171rt|- Check target journal/department style guide before finalizing register
172nm|- Psychology: APA hedging conventions; report statistics inline (F, t, p, d, η²)
173am|- History: footnote-heavy, present-tense for established facts, past-tense for events
174ri|- Computer science: passive voice more accepted; algorithm pseudocode conventions
175vj|- Education: first person accepted; reflective voice common in methodology
176th|
177df|---
178th|
179ov|## 5. Paragraph Construction Rules
180th|
181bs|### Length Variation
182mu|- Vary between 3 and 12 sentences per paragraph.
183cn|- A paragraph under 3 sentences is likely underdeveloped (unless it is a deliberate rhetorical punch).
184sw|- A paragraph over 12 sentences likely contains multiple ideas that should be split.
185th|
186oo|### Opening Strategy Variation
187na|Never start consecutive paragraphs the same way. Rotate among:
188th|
189eg|1. **Topic sentence** (most common): "A second factor complicating this analysis is…"
190mr|2. **Transition from prior point**: "This tension between cost and quality recurs in…"
191jo|3. **Question or problem**: "Why, then, do organizations persist with this approach?"
192jt|4. **Concession**: "Although these results appear robust, several conditions limit…"
193jb|5. **Illustration or case**: "The 2018 reform in Country X illustrates this dynamic."
194ia|6. **Definition or conceptual move**: "Resilience, in this framework, denotes not merely recovery but adaptive reconfiguration."
195th|
196ui|### Structural Rules
197ku|- Each paragraph must advance the argument. If removing it changes nothing, delete it.
198on|- A paragraph should not merely summarize a source; it must use the source to support a claim.
199yw|- Avoid parallel structure across paragraphs: if three consecutive paragraphs all start "X argues that… Y argues that… Z argues that…," restructure.
200th|
201io|### Example — Before and After
202nd|
203kx|Before (parallel, flat):
204hn|> Smith (2020) argues that institutional pressure drives adoption. Jones (2021) argues that resource availability mediates this effect. Chen (2022) argues that leadership style is the primary predictor.
205th|
206cm|After (varied, argumentative):
207rh|> Institutional pressure alone cannot explain the observed variation in adoption rates (Smith, 2020). Resource availability mediates the relationship: organizations with slack resources adopt faster regardless of external pressure (Jones, 2021). Yet even well-resourced organizations resist change when leadership frames the innovation as disruptive rather than enabling (Chen, 2022).
208th|
209dy|---
210th|
211yb|## 6. Citation Integration Patterns
212th|
213ru|### Signal Phrase (Narrative Integration)
214nw|Use when the author's name matters to the argument.
215br|> As Swales (1990) observes, "the Create a Research Space model…"
216th|
217hb|### Parenthetical Citation
218mr|Use when the claim, not the author, is the focus.
219rg|> Genre analysis has since expanded beyond academic writing (Bhatia, 2004; Hyland, 2006).
220th|
221zy|### Narrative Citation with Multiple Authors
222rl|> Recent work in corpus pragmatics (e.g., Ädel, 2006; McEnery & Hardie, 2012) has…
223rd|
224yb|### Block Quotes
225cm|Use when:
226su|- The exact wording matters (definitions, policy text, literary analysis)
227ph|- The passage exceeds 40 words (APA) or 4 lines (MLA)
228th|
229ae|Format:
230jw|> [Indent entire block. No quotation marks. Parenthetical citation after the final period.]
231wc|>
232vw|> (Author, Year, p. XX)
233rd|
234vu|Rules:
235db|- Block quotes are evidence, not argument. Always follow with analysis.
236ez|- Do not use block quotes to avoid paraphrasing. If you can paraphrase, paraphrase.
237th|
238cm|### Citation Density Guidelines
239bs|- Literature review: ~1–2 citations per sentence average is normal.
240mj|- Results section: cite only when comparing with prior findings.
241le|- Methodology: cite methodological sources for justification.
242ji|- Discussion: cite to connect, not to review literature again.
243dh|- Never cite without purpose: each citation must support a specific claim.
244th|
245pp|### Incomplete Citation Patterns to Avoid
246fk|- "Several studies have shown…" without naming any → either name them or delete the claim.
247jb|- "It is widely accepted that…" → cite or qualify: "In the [field] literature, X is generally treated as…"
248th|
249yi|---
250th|
251ee|## 7. Transition Strategies
252nd|
253mv|### Avoid Formulaic Transitions
254vy|Do not use "Moreover," "Furthermore," "Additionally," "In addition" as paragraph openers unless absolutely necessary. These are content-empty — they signal continuation without explaining the logical relationship.
255th|
256qa|### Content-Based Transitions Instead
257th|
258fp|| Logical Move | Weak (Formulaic) | Strong (Content-Based) |
259ru||---|---|---|
260bv|| Building on prior point | Furthermore, | This limitation becomes more consequential when… |
261wx|| Contrast | However, | Where Smith emphasizes structural constraints, Jones foregrounds… |
262es|| Concession | Nevertheless, | Even accounting for sampling bias, the effect size remains… |
263ng|| Consequence | Therefore, | If regulatory ambiguity drives this behavior, then… |
264ha|| Sequence | Next, | The second design choice — data granularity — introduces… |
265pq|| Example | For example, | The Nordic welfare states illustrate this trade-off: … |
266th|
267bf|### Principle
268ul|Every transition should tell the reader the **logical relationship** between the current and prior paragraph, not merely that one follows the other.
269th|
270ie|### Inter-Section Transitions
271qq|At the end of a section or beginning of the next, provide a one-sentence bridge:
272ps|> Having established the theoretical framework, the analysis now turns to how these concepts manifest in the empirical data.
273rd|
274st|---
275th|
276tk|## 8. Genre-Specific Conventions
277th|
278qh|### 8a. Abstract Writing
279th|
280mv|**Structured abstract** (common in empirical/science journals):
281ky|> **Background:** [1–2 sentences of context]
282ye|> **Objective:** [1 sentence stating the research question]
283uo|> **Methods:** [2–3 sentences: design, participants, analysis]
284up|> **Results:** [2–3 key findings with specifics]
285om|> **Conclusions:** [1–2 sentences: implications, not repetition]
286th|
287tm|**Unstructured abstract** (common in humanities/social science):
288wk|> Single paragraph, 150–250 words, covering: context → gap → method → key findings → significance. Write it last.
289th|
290jy|**Rules for both:**
291qn|- No citations in abstracts (unless journal requires)
292bn|- No undefined abbreviations
293in|- No hedging in the abstract that you do not use in the body — or vice versa
294bb|- Specific numbers: "reduced latency by 23%" not "significantly reduced latency"
295th|
296oh|### 8b. Introduction Funnel
297th|
298yl|```
299rh|Broad context     →  "Digital transformation is reshaping public sector service delivery."
300ds|Narrower context  →  "Within this shift, interoperability between legacy and cloud systems remains a persistent obstacle."
301ju|Specific gap      →  "While architecture-level migration patterns have been catalogued (refs), micro-level data-flow adaptation strategies have received little empirical attention."
302qu|Research question →  "This study asks: How do public agencies restructure data pipelines during incremental migration, and what organizational factors predict successful adaptation?"
303rt|Contribution      →  "We address this gap through a multi-case study of three municipal agencies, contributing an empirically grounded taxonomy of data-flow adaptation strategies."
304di|Roadmap           →  "Section 2 reviews… Section 3 describes… Section 4 presents…"
305xm|```
306th|
307hi|### 8c. Methodology Justification
308th|
309eo|Every methodological choice must answer two questions:
310kh|1. **What** did you do? (description)
311pk|2. **Why** this and not an alternative? (justification)
312th|
313fn|Template:
314mx|> "We employed [method] because [rationale]. Alternative approaches such as [alt1] and [alt2] were considered but rejected on the grounds that [reason]. This design is consistent with [theoretical/epistemological alignment] and has been used in comparable studies (refs)."
315th|
316jy|### 8d. Results Reporting
317th|
318di|- Report findings in order of research questions or hypotheses
319in|- Use tables and figures; reference them in text ("Table 3 shows…")
320bx|- Report effect sizes and confidence intervals, not just p-values
321iv|- Do not interpret here — save interpretation for Discussion
322dh|- Use past tense for completed analyses
323cg|- Example: "The mean difference was 4.3 points (SD = 1.2, 95% CI [3.1, 5.5], t(87) = 6.2, p < .001, d = 0.64)."
324th|
325fq|### 8e. Discussion Structure
326th|
327zz|Follow the interpret → connect → limit → future pattern:
328th|
329dm|1. **Interpret**: What do the findings mean? Restate key results in substantive language.
330xh|2. **Connect**: How do these results relate to prior work? Confirm, extend, contradict?
331dh|3. **Limit**: What constraints affect interpretation? (sample, method, scope, generalizability)
332gc|4. **Future**: What should researchers do next? What should practitioners do now?
333rd|
334bs|Template per finding:
335yh|> "The observed [finding] suggests that [interpretation]. This aligns with [prior work], which found [related result], but extends it by [novel contribution]. However, [limitation] constrains the generalizability of this claim. Future work should [specific next step]."
336th|
337sg|---
338th|
339vx|## 9. Common Pitfalls
340th|
341hw|### Vague Pronoun References
342hz|Before: "When the model was compared with the baseline, it performed better."
343um|After: "The model outperformed the baseline on recall (0.91 vs. 0.78)."
344th|
345ra|**Rule:** Every "it," "this," "they" must have an unambiguous antecedent. When in doubt, repeat the noun.
346th|
347mg|### Passive Voice Overuse
348bh|Before: "It was found that the algorithm was outperformed by the baseline."
349pt|After: "The baseline outperformed the algorithm."
350th|
351gu|**Guideline:** Passive is appropriate when the agent is unknown, irrelevant, or deliberately backgrounded. It is not a default. Use active voice unless passive serves a specific rhetorical purpose.
352nd|
353xh|### Nominalization
354dy|Before: "The facilitation of the improvement of student outcomes was accomplished through the implementation of peer review."
355np|After: "Peer review improved student outcomes."
356th|
357lw|**Rule:** If you can convert a nominalization to its verb form and the sentence becomes shorter and clearer, do it.
358th|
359dt|### Hedge Stacking
360is|Before: "It might perhaps possibly suggest a potential tendency toward improvement."
361gn|After: "These results suggest a modest improvement."
362nd|
363eu|**Rule:** One hedge per claim, calibrated to evidence strength (see §4).
364th|
365sh|### Claiming Too Much from Data
366wi|Before: "The data prove that the intervention works across all contexts."
367kb|After: "In this sample, the intervention produced statistically significant gains in [outcome]. Replication in other contexts is necessary."
368th|
369qs|**Rule:** Data support claims within the bounds of the study design. Extrapolation requires explicit qualification.
370th|
371lz|### Other Pitfalls
372eu|- **Straw-man literature reviews:** Do not weaken prior work to strengthen yours. Represent opposing positions at their strongest.
373ia|- **Methodology as recipe:** Do not copy-paste method sections across papers without adapting to each study's specific design.
374cd|- **Results–Discussion bleed:** If you find yourself interpreting in the Results section, move that sentence to Discussion.
375ig|- **Orphan citations:** A citation with no analytical purpose is dead weight. Remove it or give it work to do.
376th|
377ji|---
378th|
379gl|## 10. Revision Checklist
380th|
381jb|Use this checklist after completing a draft. Check each item; if it fails, revise before considering the draft done.
382nd|
383km|### Argument Coherence
384hk|- [ ] Does every section serve the central claim?
385td|- [ ] Can a reader state the thesis argument after reading only the introduction and conclusion?
386fp|- [ ] Are there sections that feel tangential? If so, integrate them or cut them.
387lp|- [ ] Does each sub-argument in the Discussion resolve or advance the main claim?
388th|
389xc|### Evidence–Citation Alignment
390wp|- [ ] Is every factual claim supported by a citation or your own data?
391ec|- [ ] Are there citations with no corresponding claim (orphan citations)?
392wv|- [ ] Do block quotes receive analysis after quotation?
393cx|- [ ] Are hedging levels appropriate to the evidence strength for each claim?
394th|
395ph|### Register Consistency
396ag|- [ ] Is the formality level consistent throughout (no sudden colloquialisms)?
397vb|- [ ] Are technical terms used consistently (no synonym-switching for variety)?
398ot|- [ ] Is person (first/third) consistent with discipline norms?
399zr|- [ ] Are tense choices consistent (past for methods/results, present for general truths)?
400th|
401pj|### Paragraph Variation
402qp|- [ ] Do paragraph lengths vary (3–12 sentences)?
403nc|- [ ] Do paragraph openings vary (no three consecutive paragraphs starting the same way)?
404qo|- [ ] Does every paragraph advance the argument (not merely summarize a source)?
405hf|- [ ] Are there paragraphs that could be split or merged?
406th|
407lw|### Transition Quality
408xf|- [ ] Have all formulaic transitions (Moreover, Furthermore, Additionally) been replaced with content-based alternatives?
409fq|- [ ] Does each paragraph opening tell the reader its logical relationship to the previous paragraph?
410nh|- [ ] Are inter-section transitions present and informative?
411th|
412fz|### Final Passes
413ni|- [ ] Read aloud to catch rhythm problems (sentences that stall, paragraphs that drag).
414uw|- [ ] Check that the abstract matches the body (no phantom claims).
415uk|- [ ] Verify all citations appear in the reference list and vice versa.
416wx|- [ ] Remove any sentence that does no work. If removing it changes nothing, it should not be there.

displayContent.text:
---
name: academic-writing
description: Templates, guidelines, and conventions for PhD-level academic writing — thesis chapters, journal papers, conference papers, and all scholarly communication.
globs:
  - "**/*"
alwaysApply: false
---

# Academic Writing Skill

## 1. Academic Writing Workflow

**Core principle: writing starts from the researcher's ideas, not from the model's imagination.**

- Always begin from the researcher's notes, outlines, data, or prior drafts. Never generate scholarly text ex nihilo.
- If the researcher provides only a vague direction, ask targeted questions before writing: What is the central claim? What evidence supports it? Who is the audience? What existing work must be engaged?
- Draft in stages: outline → argument skeleton → section drafts → revision. Do not skip to polished prose without a confirmed structure.
- When expanding researcher notes, stay faithful to their claims and emphasis. You may tighten, clarify, and organize — but do not introduce new arguments or substantive claims the researcher did not make.
- Flag any place where the text goes beyond what the researcher supplied: use `[inference]` or ask for confirmation.
- When the researcher says "write a literature review on X," the first step is to ask what sources they have or want covered, not to fabricate citations.

---

## 2. Thesis Structure Guide

### Chapter Organization

| Chapter | Purpose | Typical Length (pages) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Introduction | Establish research gap, state problem, pose questions, outline thesis | 10–20 | Funnel structure (see §8) |
| Literature Review | Map the field, identify gaps, position the study | 25–40 | Thematic or chronological; must end with a gap statement |
| Methodology | Justify and describe research design, data, and analysis | 15–30 | Must defend choices, not just describe them |
| Results | Present findings without interpretation | 20–40 | Tables, figures, descriptive statistics; raw reporting |
| Discussion | Interpret results, connect to literature, acknowledge limits | 15–30 | See discussion structure in §8 |
| Conclusion | Summarize contributions, state implications, suggest future work | 5–10 | No new evidence |

### Introduction Checklist
- [ ] Broad context: why does this area matter?
- [ ] Specific problem: what gap, contradiction, or unanswered question exists?
- [ ] Research questions or hypotheses stated explicitly
- [ ] Contribution statement: what this thesis does that prior work does not
- [ ] Thesis outline (roadmap of chapters)

### Literature Review Principles
- Organize thematically, not as an annotated list
- Every source cited must serve the argument (establish consensus, show gaps, contrast positions)
- End with an explicit gap statement: "Despite X, Y remains unaddressed…"
- Group sources by idea, not by author; avoid the "Smith (2020) found… Jones (2021) found…" pattern without synthesis

### Methodology Must Include
- Research paradigm and rationale (why this approach, not just what)
- Data collection: sources, sampling, instruments, procedures
- Analysis method and justification
- Validity/reliability/trustworthiness measures
- Ethical considerations
- Limitations acknowledged up front

---

## 3. Paper Structure Templates

### 3a. IMRAD (Empirical / Experimental)

```
Abstract (150–250 words, structured or unstructured)
1. Introduction
   - Context and significance
   - Literature positioning
   - Research question / hypothesis
   - Paper organization
2. Methods
   - Participants / Materials / Procedure
   - Analytical approach
   - Justification for each choice
3. Results
   - Findings organized by research question
   - Tables and figures with descriptive captions
   - No interpretation here
4. Discussion
   - Interpret each finding
   - Connect to prior literature
   - Limitations
   - Implications and future directions
5. Conclusion (sometimes merged with Discussion)
References
Appendices (if any)
```

### 3b. Argument-Driven (Theoretical / Analytical)

```
Abstract (150–250 words)
1. Introduction
   - Problem statement
   - Why existing frameworks fall short
   - Thesis statement (the argument)
2. Conceptual / Theoretical Grounding
   - Key concepts defined
   - Prior frameworks analyzed
   - Gaps or tensions identified
3. Analysis (may be multiple sections)
   - Each section advances one sub-argument
   - Evidence: textual, logical, case-based, comparative
4. Discussion
   - Synthesize sub-arguments into main claim
   - Respond to anticipated objections
   - Limitations
5. Conclusion
   - Restate contribution
   - Broader implications
References
```

### 3c. Systematic Review

```
Abstract (structured: Background, Objectives, Methods, Results, Conclusions)
1. Introduction
   - Rationale for the review
   - Review questions
2. Methods
   - Search strategy (databases, terms, date range)
   - Inclusion/exclusion criteria
   - Screening process (PRISMA flow)
   - Data extraction and synthesis method
   - Quality appraisal
3. Results
   - Search outcomes
   - Study characteristics (summary table)
   - Synthesis by theme/question
   - Quality assessment results
4. Discussion
   - Summary of findings
   - Comparison with prior reviews
   - Strengths and limitations of the review
   - Implications for practice and research
5. Conclusion
References
Appendices (search strings, full extraction tables)
```

---

## 4. Academic Register Principles

### Precision
- Use the most specific term available: "three-item scale" not "a brief measure"; "statistically significant at α = .01" not "highly significant"
- Define technical terms on first use; do not assume disciplinary consensus on meaning

### Hedging (Calibrated by Claim Strength)

| Claim Strength | Typical Hedge | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Strong (direct evidence) | indicates, demonstrates | "The results indicate that…" |
| Moderate (correlational) | suggests, is associated with | "This pattern suggests a relationship between…" |
| Weak (speculative) | may, could, might, appears to | "These findings may reflect…" |
| Very weak (exploratory) | it is possible that, one interpretation is | "One possible interpretation is that…" |

**Rules:**
- Never hedge a direct replication: if you replicated a known result, say so directly.
- Never claim more than the data supports: "proves" is almost always wrong; "establishes" only for deductive arguments.
- Avoid hedge stacking: "It might perhaps possibly suggest…" — pick one calibrated term.

### Formal but Not Stiff
- Prefer "we argue" over "it is argued by the authors"
- Prefer "the data show" over "it can be seen that the data show"
- Contractions are genre-dependent: avoid in formal journal articles; acceptable in some conference papers and most theses after supervisor approval
- Avoid ornamentalLatin: "e.g." and "i.e." are fine; "ibid.," "op. cit.," and "viz." add friction without clarity

### Discipline-Specific Conventions
- Check target journal/department style guide before finalizing register
- Psychology: APA hedging conventions; report statistics inline (F, t, p, d, η²)
- History: footnote-heavy, present-tense for established facts, past-tense for events
- Computer science: passive voice more accepted; algorithm pseudocode conventions
- Education: first person accepted; reflective voice common in methodology

---

## 5. Paragraph Construction Rules

### Length Variation
- Vary between 3 and 12 sentences per paragraph.
- A paragraph under 3 sentences is likely underdeveloped (unless it is a deliberate rhetorical punch).
- A paragraph over 12 sentences likely contains multiple ideas that should be split.

### Opening Strategy Variation
Never start consecutive paragraphs the same way. Rotate among:

1. **Topic sentence** (most common): "A second factor complicating this analysis is…"
2. **Transition from prior point**: "This tension between cost and quality recurs in…"
3. **Question or problem**: "Why, then, do organizations persist with this approach?"
4. **Concession**: "Although these results appear robust, several conditions limit…"
5. **Illustration or case**: "The 2018 reform in Country X illustrates this dynamic."
6. **Definition or conceptual move**: "Resilience, in this framework, denotes not merely recovery but adaptive reconfiguration."

### Structural Rules
- Each paragraph must advance the argument. If removing it changes nothing, delete it.
- A paragraph should not merely summarize a source; it must use the source to support a claim.
- Avoid parallel structure across paragraphs: if three consecutive paragraphs all start "X argues that… Y argues that… Z argues that…," restructure.

### Example — Before and After

Before (parallel, flat):
> Smith (2020) argues that institutional pressure drives adoption. Jones (2021) argues that resource availability mediates this effect. Chen (2022) argues that leadership style is the primary predictor.

After (varied, argumentative):
> Institutional pressure alone cannot explain the observed variation in adoption rates (Smith, 2020). Resource availability mediates the relationship: organizations with slack resources adopt faster regardless of external pressure (Jones, 2021). Yet even well-resourced organizations resist change when leadership frames the innovation as disruptive rather than enabling (Chen, 2022).

---

## 6. Citation Integration Patterns

### Signal Phrase (Narrative Integration)
Use when the author's name matters to the argument.
> As Swales (1990) observes, "the Create a Research Space model…"

### Parenthetical Citation
Use when the claim, not the author, is the focus.
> Genre analysis has since expanded beyond academic writing (Bhatia, 2004; Hyland, 2006).

### Narrative Citation with Multiple Authors
> Recent work in corpus pragmatics (e.g., Ädel, 2006; McEnery & Hardie, 2012) has…

### Block Quotes
Use when:
- The exact wording matters (definitions, policy text, literary analysis)
- The passage exceeds 40 words (APA) or 4 lines (MLA)

Format:
> [Indent entire block. No quotation marks. Parenthetical citation after the final period.]
>
> (Author, Year, p. XX)

Rules:
- Block quotes are evidence, not argument. Always follow with analysis.
- Do not use block quotes to avoid paraphrasing. If you can paraphrase, paraphrase.

### Citation Density Guidelines
- Literature review: ~1–2 citations per sentence average is normal.
- Results section: cite only when comparing with prior findings.
- Methodology: cite methodological sources for justification.
- Discussion: cite to connect, not to review literature again.
- Never cite without purpose: each citation must support a specific claim.

### Incomplete Citation Patterns to Avoid
- "Several studies have shown…" without naming any → either name them or delete the claim.
- "It is widely accepted that…" → cite or qualify: "In the [field] literature, X is generally treated as…"

---

## 7. Transition Strategies

### Avoid Formulaic Transitions
Do not use "Moreover," "Furthermore," "Additionally," "In addition" as paragraph openers unless absolutely necessary. These are content-empty — they signal continuation without explaining the logical relationship.

### Content-Based Transitions Instead

| Logical Move | Weak (Formulaic) | Strong (Content-Based) |
|---|---|---|
| Building on prior point | Furthermore, | This limitation becomes more consequential when… |
| Contrast | However, | Where Smith emphasizes structural constraints, Jones foregrounds… |
| Concession | Nevertheless, | Even accounting for sampling bias, the effect size remains… |
| Consequence | Therefore, | If regulatory ambiguity drives this behavior, then… |
| Sequence | Next, | The second design choice — data granularity — introduces… |
| Example | For example, | The Nordic welfare states illustrate this trade-off: … |

### Principle
Every transition should tell the reader the **logical relationship** between the current and prior paragraph, not merely that one follows the other.

### Inter-Section Transitions
At the end of a section or beginning of the next, provide a one-sentence bridge:
> Having established the theoretical framework, the analysis now turns to how these concepts manifest in the empirical data.

---

## 8. Genre-Specific Conventions

### 8a. Abstract Writing

**Structured abstract** (common in empirical/science journals):
> **Background:** [1–2 sentences of context]
> **Objective:** [1 sentence stating the research question]
> **Methods:** [2–3 sentences: design, participants, analysis]
> **Results:** [2–3 key findings with specifics]
> **Conclusions:** [1–2 sentences: implications, not repetition]

**Unstructured abstract** (common in humanities/social science):
> Single paragraph, 150–250 words, covering: context → gap → method → key findings → significance. Write it last.

**Rules for both:**
- No citations in abstracts (unless journal requires)
- No undefined abbreviations
- No hedging in the abstract that you do not use in the body — or vice versa
- Specific numbers: "reduced latency by 23%" not "significantly reduced latency"

### 8b. Introduction Funnel

```
Broad context     →  "Digital transformation is reshaping public sector service delivery."
Narrower context  →  "Within this shift, interoperability between legacy and cloud systems remains a persistent obstacle."
Specific gap      →  "While architecture-level migration patterns have been catalogued (refs), micro-level data-flow adaptation strategies have received little empirical attention."
Research question →  "This study asks: How do public agencies restructure data pipelines during incremental migration, and what organizational factors predict successful adaptation?"
Contribution      →  "We address this gap through a multi-case study of three municipal agencies, contributing an empirically grounded taxonomy of data-flow adaptation strategies."
Roadmap           →  "Section 2 reviews… Section 3 describes… Section 4 presents…"
```

### 8c. Methodology Justification

Every methodological choice must answer two questions:
1. **What** did you do? (description)
2. **Why** this and not an alternative? (justification)

Template:
> "We employed [method] because [rationale]. Alternative approaches such as [alt1] and [alt2] were considered but rejected on the grounds that [reason]. This design is consistent with [theoretical/epistemological alignment] and has been used in comparable studies (refs)."

### 8d. Results Reporting

- Report findings in order of research questions or hypotheses
- Use tables and figures; reference them in text ("Table 3 shows…")
- Report effect sizes and confidence intervals, not just p-values
- Do not interpret here — save interpretation for Discussion
- Use past tense for completed analyses
- Example: "The mean difference was 4.3 points (SD = 1.2, 95% CI [3.1, 5.5], t(87) = 6.2, p < .001, d = 0.64)."

### 8e. Discussion Structure

Follow the interpret → connect → limit → future pattern:

1. **Interpret**: What do the findings mean? Restate key results in substantive language.
2. **Connect**: How do these results relate to prior work? Confirm, extend, contradict?
3. **Limit**: What constraints affect interpretation? (sample, method, scope, generalizability)
4. **Future**: What should researchers do next? What should practitioners do now?

Template per finding:
> "The observed [finding] suggests that [interpretation]. This aligns with [prior work], which found [related result], but extends it by [novel contribution]. However, [limitation] constrains the generalizability of this claim. Future work should [specific next step]."

---

## 9. Common Pitfalls

### Vague Pronoun References
Before: "When the model was compared with the baseline, it performed better."
After: "The model outperformed the baseline on recall (0.91 vs. 0.78)."

**Rule:** Every "it," "this," "they" must have an unambiguous antecedent. When in doubt, repeat the noun.

### Passive Voice Overuse
Before: "It was found that the algorithm was outperformed by the baseline."
After: "The baseline outperformed the algorithm."

**Guideline:** Passive is appropriate when the agent is unknown, irrelevant, or deliberately backgrounded. It is not a default. Use active voice unless passive serves a specific rhetorical purpose.

### Nominalization
Before: "The facilitation of the improvement of student outcomes was accomplished through the implementation of peer review."
After: "Peer review improved student outcomes."

**Rule:** If you can convert a nominalization to its verb form and the sentence becomes shorter and clearer, do it.

### Hedge Stacking
Before: "It might perhaps possibly suggest a potential tendency toward improvement."
After: "These results suggest a modest improvement."

**Rule:** One hedge per claim, calibrated to evidence strength (see §4).

### Claiming Too Much from Data
Before: "The data prove that the intervention works across all contexts."
After: "In this sample, the intervention produced statistically significant gains in [outcome]. Replication in other contexts is necessary."

**Rule:** Data support claims within the bounds of the study design. Extrapolation requires explicit qualification.

### Other Pitfalls
- **Straw-man literature reviews:** Do not weaken prior work to strengthen yours. Represent opposing positions at their strongest.
- **Methodology as recipe:** Do not copy-paste method sections across papers without adapting to each study's specific design.
- **Results–Discussion bleed:** If you find yourself interpreting in the Results section, move that sentence to Discussion.
- **Orphan citations:** A citation with no analytical purpose is dead weight. Remove it or give it work to do.

---

## 10. Revision Checklist

Use this checklist after completing a draft. Check each item; if it fails, revise before considering the draft done.

### Argument Coherence
- [ ] Does every section serve the central claim?
- [ ] Can a reader state the thesis argument after reading only the introduction and conclusion?
- [ ] Are there sections that feel tangential? If so, integrate them or cut them.
- [ ] Does each sub-argument in the Discussion resolve or advance the main claim?

### Evidence–Citation Alignment
- [ ] Is every factual claim supported by a citation or your own data?
- [ ] Are there citations with no corresponding claim (orphan citations)?
- [ ] Do block quotes receive analysis after quotation?
- [ ] Are hedging levels appropriate to the evidence strength for each claim?

### Register Consistency
- [ ] Is the formality level consistent throughout (no sudden colloquialisms)?
- [ ] Are technical terms used consistently (no synonym-switching for variety)?
- [ ] Is person (first/third) consistent with discipline norms?
- [ ] Are tense choices consistent (past for methods/results, present for general truths)?

### Paragraph Variation
- [ ] Do paragraph lengths vary (3–12 sentences)?
- [ ] Do paragraph openings vary (no three consecutive paragraphs starting the same way)?
- [ ] Does every paragraph advance the argument (not merely summarize a source)?
- [ ] Are there paragraphs that could be split or merged?

### Transition Quality
- [ ] Have all formulaic transitions (Moreover, Furthermore, Additionally) been replaced with content-based alternatives?
- [ ] Does each paragraph opening tell the reader its logical relationship to the previous paragraph?
- [ ] Are inter-section transitions present and informative?

### Final Passes
- [ ] Read aloud to catch rhythm problems (sentences that stall, paragraphs that drag).
- [ ] Check that the abstract matches the body (no phantom claims).
- [ ] Verify all citations appear in the reference list and vice versa.
- [ ] Remove any sentence that does no work. If removing it changes nothing, it should not be there.

content.text:
1lh|---
2eu|name: literature-search
3mr|description: Systematic search strategies for academic databases — Semantic Scholar, arXiv, CrossRef, OpenAlex, Google Scholar — with API usage patterns and citation tracking.
4qn|globs:
5di|  - "**/*"
6oi|alwaysApply: false
7mu|---
8th|
9rz|# Literature Search
10th|
11ot|## 1. Search Strategy Overview
12th|
13iv|Every search begins with a clearly defined research question. Skipping this produces noisy results and wasted effort.
14th|
15lj|**Process:**
16th|
17uo|1. **Define the research question** — Write it as a focused, answerable question. If you cannot state it in one sentence, it is too broad.
18nm|2. **Extract keywords** — Identify the core concepts (2–5 terms). For each concept, list synonyms, broader terms, narrower terms, and discipline-specific variants.
19ps|3. **Construct boolean queries** — Combine terms using operators (see §3). Start narrow, broaden only when results are sparse.
20nx|4. **Select databases** — Match databases to the domain: CS → arXiv + Semantic Scholar; biomed → CrossRef + OpenAlex; interdisciplinary → all five.
21wh|5. **Execute searches** — Run queries, log everything (see §5), assess relevance.
22dg|6. **Chain citations** — Follow the citation graph forward and backward from seed papers (see §4).
23dd|7. **Deduplicate** — Merge results across databases (see §6).
24ro|8. **Evaluate quality** — Filter by source credibility (see §7).
25hf|9. **Document** — Record every paper in the citation database (see §8).
26hq|10. **Stop** — Apply saturation criteria (see §10).
27th|
28mz|**Never search without a logged query.** If you cannot reproduce your search later, it did not happen.
29th|
30ma|---
31st|
32ku|## 2. Database-Specific Search Instructions
33rd|
34fa|### 2.1 Semantic Scholar
35th|
36qp|**Base URL:** `https://api.semanticscholar.org/graph/v1`
37th|
38pp|**Rate limits:** 100 requests/5 min (unauthenticated). Register for API key at https://www.semanticscholar.org/product/api#api-key for higher limits.
39th|
40sv|**Search papers:**
41st|
42pc|```bash
43iv|curl -s "https://api.semanticscholar.org/graph/v1/paper/search?query=transformer+attention+mechanism&limit=10&fields=title,authors,year,abstract,citationCount,externalIds,url" | jq .
44xo|```
45th|
46xy|**Paper details by Semantic Scholar ID:**
47th|
48pc|```bash
49nv|curl -s "https://api.semanticscholar.org/graph/v1/paper/649def34f8be52c8b66281af98ae884c09aef38b?fields=title,authors,year,abstract,citationCount,references,citations,externalIds" | jq .
50sq|```
51st|
52fh|**Paper details by DOI:**
53rd|
54pc|```bash
55wg|curl -s "https://api.semanticscholar.org/graph/v1/paper/DOI:10.1038/nature12373?fields=title,authors,year,abstract,citationCount,externalIds" | jq .
56zy|```
57th|
58xm|**Paper details by arXiv ID:**
59th|
60pc|```bash
61wa|curl -s "https://api.semanticscholar.org/graph/v1/paper/ARXIV:1706.03762?fields=title,authors,year,abstract,citationCount" | jq .
62fo|```
63rd|
64ji|**Backward citations (references — who this paper cites):**
65th|
66pc|```bash
67jd|curl -s "https://api.semanticscholar.org/graph/v1/paper/649def34f8be52c8b66281af98ae884c09aef38b/references?fields=title,authors,year,citationCount,externalIds&limit=100" | jq .
68oa|```
69th|
70ts|**Forward citations (who cites this paper):**
71st|
72pc|```bash
73sb|curl -s "https://api.semanticscholar.org/graph/v1/paper/649def34f8be52c8b66281af98ae884c09aef38b/citations?fields=title,authors,year,citationCount,externalIds&limit=100" | jq .
74ux|```
75th|
76kn|**Author search:**
77th|
78pc|```bash
79bf|curl -s "https://api.semanticscholar.org/graph/v1/author/search?query=Ashish+Vaswani&limit=5" | jq .
80am|```
81st|
82an|**Author details and papers:**
83rd|
84pc|```bash
85ft|curl -s "https://api.semanticscholar.org/graph/v1/author/1741102?fields=name,paperCount,hIndex,papers.title,papers.year,papers.citationCount" | jq .
86ze|```
87th|
88yh|**Bulk search (for systematic reviews):**
89th|
90pc|```bash
91zf|curl -s -X POST "https://api.semanticscholar.org/graph/v1/paper/search/bulk" \
92js|  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
93oa|  -d '{"query":"neural machine translation","fields":"title,authors,year,abstract,citationCount,externalIds"}' | jq .
94fn|```
95th|
96ri|**Key fields to request:** `title`, `authors`, `year`, `abstract`, `citationCount`, `externalIds` (contains DOI, arXivId, PubMedId), `url`, `venue`, `publicationTypes`.
97th|
98wf|---
99th|
100mz|### 2.2 arXiv
101st|
102nf|**Base URL:** `http://export.arxiv.org/api/query`
103rd|
104pm|**Rate limits:** 1 request per 3 seconds. Respect this strictly — arXiv will block aggressive clients.
105th|
106sv|**Search papers:**
107th|
108pc|```bash
109xf|curl -s "http://export.arxiv.org/api/query?search_query=all:transformer+AND+all:attention&start=0&max_results=10&sortBy=relevance&sortOrder=descending" | xmllint --format -
110dk|```
111th|
112av|**Query syntax:**
113hn|- Prefixes: `ti` (title), `au` (author), `abs` (abstract), `cat` (category), `all` (all fields)
114co|- Operators: `AND`, `OR`, `ANDNOT`
115xm|- Grouping: parentheses
116th|
117pc|```bash
118yf|# Title search
119ai|curl -s "http://export.arxiv.org/api/query?search_query=ti:%22attention+is+all+you+need%22&max_results=5" | xmllint --format -
120th|
121yp|# Author search
122ja|curl -s "http://export.arxiv.org/api/query?search_query=au:Vaswani&max_results=10" | xmllint --format -
123rd|
124pz|# Category filter (cs.CL = Computation and Language)
125ag|curl -s "http://export.arxiv.org/api/query?search_query=cat:cs.CL+AND+ti:transformer&max_results=10" | xmllint --format -
126th|
127cr|# Complex boolean
128an|curl -s "http://export.arxiv.org/api/query?search_query=(ti:transformer+OR+ti:attention)+ANDNOT+cat:physics&max_results=10" | xmllint --format -
129kx|```
130th|
131si|**Common arXiv categories:**
132oi|- `cs.AI` — Artificial Intelligence
133rp|- `cs.CL` — Computation and Language
134kv|- `cs.CV` — Computer Vision
135lw|- `cs.LG` — Machine Learning
136ix|- `cs.NE` — Neural and Evolutionary Computing
137mc|- `stat.ML` — Machine Learning (Statistics)
138th|
139cc|**Parsing XML responses** — Use `jq` after converting with `xq` (from `yq` package) or parse directly:
140th|
141pc|```bash
142sp|curl -s "http://export.arxiv.org/api/query?search_query=ti:transformer&max_results=5" | \
143rf|  xq '.feed.entry[] | {id: .id, title: .title, summary: .summary, authors: [.author[].name], published: .published}'
144px|```
145th|
146qa|**Get paper by arXiv ID:**
147th|
148pc|```bash
149xp|curl -s "http://export.arxiv.org/api/query?id_list=1706.03762" | xmllint --format -
150zg|```
151st|
152kg|**Pagination:** Use `start` and `max_results`. Maximum `max_results` is 2000 per request. Total results in `<opensearch:totalResults>`.
153rd|
154vj|---
155th|
156hn|### 2.3 CrossRef
157th|
158rt|**Base URL:** `https://api.crossref.org`
159th|
160qt|**Rate limits:** 50 requests/second (with polite pool header). Without the header: heavily rate-limited. **Always include the `mailto` parameter.**
161st|
162ha|**Search works:**
163rd|
164pc|```bash
165wb|curl -s "https://api.crossref.org/works?query=transformer+attention+mechanism&rows=10&mailto=your-email@example.com" | jq .
166xa|```
167th|
168en|**DOI lookup:**
169th|
170pc|```bash
171nm|curl -s "https://api.crossref.org/works/10.1038/nature12373?mailto=your-email@example.com" | jq .
172ud|```
173rd|
174tm|**Filter syntax** — Separate multiple filters with commas. Use `funders`, `from-pub-date`, `type`, `has-abstract`, etc.
175th|
176pc|```bash
177sr|# Filter by publication date range and type
178pf|curl -s "https://api.crossref.org/works?query=neural+networks&filter=from-pub-date:2020,type:journal-article&rows=20&mailto=your-email@example.com" | jq .
179th|
180kh|# Filter by funder (e.g., NSF)
181ze|curl -s "https://api.crossref.org/works?filter=funder:10.13039/100000001&rows=10&mailto=your-email@example.com" | jq .
182nd|
183hw|# Only works with abstracts
184gc|curl -s "https://api.crossref.org/works?query=transformer&filter=has-abstract:true&rows=10&mailto=your-email@example.com" | jq .
185jj|```
186th|
187ui|**Faceted search** — Get breakdown of results by a facet (year, journal, funder):
188th|
189pc|```bash
190qd|curl -s "https://api.crossref.org/works?query=transformer&facet=container-title:*,published-date:*&rows=0&mailto=your-email@example.com" | jq '.message.facets'
191qy|```
192nd|
193pv|**Sort options:** `score` (relevance), `published-date`, `is-referenced-by-count` (citations), `deposited-date`.
194th|
195pc|```bash
196vr|curl -s "https://api.crossref.org/works?query=deep+learning&sort=is-referenced-by-count&order=desc&rows=10&mailto=your-email@example.com" | jq .
197jb|```
198th|
199oz|**Key response fields:** `DOI`, `title`, `author`, `published-print`, `published-online`, `container-title` (journal), `is-referenced-by-count` (citations received), `references` (if available), `link` (URLs to full text).
200th|
201ph|---
202nd|
203ut|### 2.4 OpenAlex
204th|
205uf|**Base URL:** `https://api.openalex.org`
206th|
207im|**Rate limits:** 10 requests/second. Include `mailto` parameter for the polite pool (faster, more reliable).
208th|
209ha|**Search works:**
210th|
211pc|```bash
212ox|curl -s "https://api.openalex.org/works?search=transformer+attention+mechanism&per_page=10&mailto=your-email@example.com" | jq .
213rc|```
214th|
215rv|**Filter syntax** — Use `filter` parameter with comma-separated `key:value` pairs:
216th|
217pc|```bash
218td|# Filter by concept/level
219jj|curl -s "https://api.openalex.org/works?filter=concepts.id:C154945302,from_publication_date:2020-01-01&per_page=10&mailto=your-email@example.com" | jq .
220th|
221zz|# Filter by author
222kh|curl -s "https://api.openalex.org/works?filter=author.id:A5023848391&per_page=10&mailto=your-email@example.com" | jq .
223rd|
224ej|# Filter by type and open access
225bn|curl -s "https://api.openalex.org/works?filter=type:article,is_oa:true&search=machine+learning&per_page=10&mailto=your-email@example.com" | jq .
226th|
227nl|# Citation count filter
228xr|curl -s "https://api.openalex.org/works?filter=cited_by_count:>100,from_publication_date:2022-01-01&per_page=10&mailto=your-email@example.com" | jq .
229qy|```
230th|
231me|**Concept IDs** — Find concept IDs by searching:
232nd|
233pc|```bash
234my|curl -s "https://api.openalex.org/concepts?search=machine+learning&per_page=5&mailto=your-email@example.com" | jq '.results[] | {id, display_name, level, works_count}'
235mu|```
236th|
237kn|**Author search:**
238th|
239pc|```bash
240ja|curl -s "https://api.openalex.org/authors?search=Ashish+Vaswani&per_page=5&mailto=your-email@example.com" | jq '.results[] | {id, display_name, works_count, cited_by_count, h_index}'
241gx|```
242nd|
243js|**Citation chaining — backward (referenced works):**
244th|
245pc|```bash
246gi|curl -s "https://api.openalex.org/works/W4242298634?select=id,title,referenced_works&mailto=your-email@example.com" | jq .
247dd|# Then fetch the referenced works in bulk
248ll|curl -s "https://api.openalex.org/works?filter=openalex:W4242298634|W1234567890|W9876543210&per_page=50&mailto=your-email@example.com" | jq .
249gc|```
250th|
251zl|**Citation chaining — forward (citing works):**
252nd|
253pc|```bash
254xa|curl -s "https://api.openalex.org/works?filter=cites:W4242298634&per_page=25&mailto=your-email@example.com" | jq .
255wx|```
256th|
257zz|**Key fields:** `id` (OpenAlex ID), `doi`, `title`, `authorships`, `concepts`, `cited_by_count`, `referenced_works`, `cited_by_api_url`, `type`, `publication_year`, `open_access`.
258th|
259rx|---
260th|
261md|### 2.5 Google Scholar
262nd|
263wr|Google Scholar has no official API. Use one of these approaches:
264th|
265cc|**Option A: `scholarly` Python library (free, rate-limited, may require proxy)**
266th|
267pc|```bash
268fl|pip install scholarly
269th|
270us|python3 -c "
271pj|from scholarly import scholarly
272ow|results = scholarly.search_pubs('transformer attention mechanism')
273rk|for i, r in enumerate(results):
274fg|    if i >= 10: break
275ak|    print(f\"Title: {r['bib']['title']}\")
276rb|    print(f\"  Year: {r['bib'].get('pub_year', 'N/A')}\")
277qy|    print(f\"  Citations: {r.get('num_citations', 0)}\")
278hy|    print(f\"  URL: {r.get('pub_url', 'N/A')}\")
279ty|    print()
280zg|"
281eg|```
282nd|
283fl|**Option B: SerpAPI (paid, reliable, structured JSON)**
284th|
285pc|```bash
286tf|curl -s "https://serpapi.com/search?engine=google_scholar&q=transformer+attention+mechanism&num=10&api_key=YOUR_SERPAPI_KEY" | jq '.organic_results[] | {title, result_id, snippet, cited_by, pub_info}'
287ot|```
288th|
289gx|**Option C: `read` tool** — For individual papers found via other databases, use the `read` tool on the Scholar URL to extract metadata. This avoids scraping.
290th|
291pc|```bash
292la|# Example: read a Google Scholar result page
293ty|# Use the read tool with the Scholar search URL
294ne|```
295th|
296pg|**Important caveats for Google Scholar:**
297vx|- Rate limiting is aggressive. Use delays of 10–30 seconds between requests.
298bb|- `scholarly` may trigger CAPTCHAs. Use a proxy or the `use_proxy` option.
299fg|- Scholar metadata is less structured than other sources — always cross-reference DOI with CrossRef or Semantic Scholar.
300no|- Scholar does not expose a DOI directly; extract it from the snippet and verify.
301st|
302tr|---
303rd|
304yy|## 3. Boolean Query Construction
305th|
306xd|| Operator | Meaning | Example |
307oh||----------|---------|---------|
308gb|| `AND` | Both terms must appear | `transformer AND attention` |
309de|| `OR` | Either term appears | `neural OR deep learning` |
310dj|| `NOT` | Exclude term | `apple NOT fruit` |
311gy|| `"..."` | Exact phrase | `"attention is all you need"` |
312ke|| `*` | Wildcard (varies by DB) | `transform*` (transform, transformers, transformation) |
313uu|| `NEAR/n` | Within n words (CrossRef) | `machine NEAR/3 learning` |
314th|
315wf|**Database-specific syntax:**
316th|
317ej|| Database | AND | OR | NOT | Phrase | Wildcard |
318lh||----------|-----|----|-----|--------|----------|
319fi|| Semantic Scholar | `+` (space-separated) | `+OR+` | N/A | `"..."` | `*` |
320fg|| arXiv | `AND` | `OR` | `ANDNOT` | `"..."` | `*` (limited) |
321yg|| CrossRef | space (implicit AND) | `|` (in filters) | N/A | `"..."` | `*` |
322dx|| OpenAlex | space (implicit AND) | `|` (in filter values) | `-` (exclude) | `"..."` | `*` |
323rd|
324nj|**Query refinement pattern:**
325th|
326cw|1. Start with the core terms: `transformer AND attention`
327fs|2. If too many results: add specificity: `transformer AND "attention mechanism" AND "neural network"`
328tf|3. If too few: broaden with synonyms: `(transformer OR "self-attention") AND (neural OR deep)`
329ib|4. If still too few: remove the least important term or use OR extensively
330vc|5. If off-topic noise: add NOT terms: `transformer AND attention NOT power NOT electrical`
331st|
332hj|---
333rd|
334vz|## 4. Citation Chaining Strategy
335th|
336hl|### Backward Chaining (who does this paper cite?)
337th|
338zh|Start with a seed paper and trace its references. This finds foundational work.
339th|
340pc|```bash
341et|# Semantic Scholar
342rz|SEED_ID="649def34f8be52c8b66281af98ae884c09aef38b"
343yy|curl -s "https://api.semanticscholar.org/graph/v1/paper/$SEED_ID/references?fields=title,authors,year,citationCount,externalIds&limit=100" | jq '.data[].citedPaper | select(.citationCount > 100) | {title, year, citationCount, doi: .externalIds.DOI}'
344th|
345xo|# OpenAlex
346bb|SEED_OA="W4242298634"
347ly|curl -s "https://api.openalex.org/works?filter=cites:$SEED_OA&per_page=25&mailto=your-email@example.com" | jq '.results[] | {title, doi, cited_by_count}'
348ea|```
349th|
350vg|### Forward Chaining (who cites this paper?)
351st|
352ma|Find recent work that builds on the seed. This finds state-of-the-art.
353rd|
354pc|```bash
355et|# Semantic Scholar
356fs|curl -s "https://api.semanticscholar.org/graph/v1/paper/$SEED_ID/citations?fields=title,authors,year,citationCount,externalIds&limit=100" | jq '.data[].citingPaper | select(.year > 2022) | {title, year, citationCount}'
357th|
358xo|# OpenAlex
359xh|curl -s "https://api.openalex.org/works?filter=cites:$SEED_OA&per_page=25&sort=cited_by_count:desc&mailto=your-email@example.com" | jq '.results[] | {title, publication_year, cited_by_count}'
360cj|```
361st|
362uo|### Snowball Search Pattern
363rd|
364sp|1. Start with 3–5 seed papers from initial keyword search.
365vz|2. For each seed: backward chain → collect high-citation references.
366jc|3. For each seed: forward chain → collect recent high-citation citing works.
367dt|4. Add newly discovered papers as seeds. Repeat.
368vn|5. Stop when new iterations yield <5 novel papers not already in your database.
369th|
370as|---
371st|
372yh|## 5. Search Documentation
373rd|
374si|**Always log searches.** Create a search log alongside your citation database.
375th|
376hv|**Format** — append to `literature/search-log.md`:
377th|
378fr|```markdown
379df|## Search: [YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM]
380th|
381oc|**Research question:** [your question]
382ji|**Database:** [Semantic Scholar | arXiv | CrossRef | OpenAlex | Google Scholar]
383kk|**Query:** [exact query string used]
384fr|**Filters:** [any filters applied]
385cv|**Results:** [count] total, [count] screened, [count] included
386ex|**Notes:** [observations about result quality, gaps, adjustments needed]
387wl|```
388th|
389hk|**Why this matters:**
390wl|- Systematic reviews require reproducible search strategies.
391eg|- You will forget what you searched and what you missed.
392ep|- Future-you (or a reviewer) needs to verify coverage.
393rd|
394yy|---
395th|
396cp|## 6. Deduplication
397th|
398uz|When aggregating across databases, deduplicate before screening.
399th|
400zy|**Priority order for deduplication keys:**
401st|
402vy|1. **DOI** — most reliable. Normalize by lowercasing and removing `https://doi.org/` prefix.
403oy|2. **Semantic Scholar paper ID** — stable, unique, covers papers without DOI.
404dd|3. **arXiv ID** — stable for preprints. Combine with DOI when both exist.
405jk|4. **Title similarity** — last resort. Use fuzzy matching (Levenshtein ratio > 0.85). Normalize first: lowercase, strip punctuation, collapse whitespace.
406th|
407pc|```bash
408mu|# Deduplicate by DOI from a JSON results file
409jx|cat results.json | jq -s '[.[][] | {doi: .externalIds.DOI, s2id: .paperId, title: .title}] | unique_by(.doi // .s2id // .title)'
410kk|```
411th|
412ed|**Workflow:**
413hx|1. Collect all results into a single JSON file.
414zc|2. Sort by deduplication key priority.
415rt|3. For each paper, check if a matching key already exists in the citation database.
416lr|4. If exists: merge metadata (keep richer abstract, add missing fields).
417mt|5. If new: add to database.
418th|
419cf|---
420th|
421sg|## 7. Source Quality Evaluation
422nd|
423xc|**Tier 1 — Strong evidence:**
424fu|- Peer-reviewed journal articles (verified via CrossRef journal metadata)
425pk|- Conference proceedings from top venues (check acceptance rates)
426ae|- Preprints with significant citation counts (>50 for CS, >20 for niche fields)
427an|- Authors with h-index > 20 in the relevant field
428th|
429wv|**Tier 2 — Moderate evidence:**
430qs|- Preprints with some citations (10–50)
431dj|- Workshop papers, extended abstracts
432wi|- Authors with h-index 10–20
433og|- Industry technical reports (e.g., Google Research, Meta AI)
434th|
435ii|**Tier 3 — Use with caution:**
436er|- Un-cited preprints (unless very recent, <6 months)
437ya|- Non-peer-reviewed blog posts, Medium articles
438bh|- Conference papers at low-selectivity venues
439th|
440yu|**Evaluation checklist per paper:**
441st|
442um|| Criterion | How to check |
443cy||-----------|-------------|
444ib|| Citation count | Semantic Scholar `citationCount`, CrossRef `is-referenced-by-count`, OpenAlex `cited_by_count` |
445me|| Journal impact | CrossRef `container-title`, look up JCR/SJR |
446pm|| Author h-index | Semantic Scholar author endpoint, OpenAlex author `h_index` |
447uy|| Recency | `publication_year` or `year` field |
448bm|| Methodology rigor | Read abstract/methods section — check for controlled experiments, sample size, reproducibility |
449im|| Reproducibility | Check for code/data availability, registered reports |
450th|
451cv|---
452nd|
453qc|## 8. Citation Database Template
454th|
455mj|Store citations in `literature/citations.md` with YAML frontmatter per entry.
456th|
457hl|**Template:**
458th|
459fr|```markdown
460qd|---
461lm|citation_key: vaswani2017attention
462um|authors:
463xy|  - Vaswani, Ashish
464yl|  - Shazeer, Noam
465ne|  - Parmar, Niki
466xp|  - Uszkoreit, Jakob
467or|  - Jones, Llion
468ka|  - Gomez, Aidan N.
469pr|  - Kaiser, Lukasz
470vl|  - Polosukhin, Illia
471zp|title: "Attention Is All You Need"
472ny|year: 2017
473iu|source: NeurIPS
474fx|doi: "10.48550/arXiv.1706.03762"
475wp|url: "https://arxiv.org/abs/1706.03762"
476ly|abstract: >
477yy|  The dominant sequence transduction models are based on complex recurrent or
478ug|  convolutional neural networks. We propose a new simple network architecture,
479xz|  the Transformer, based solely on attention mechanisms...
480qy|cited_because: Foundation paper for transformer architecture; essential background for any work on attention mechanisms.
481qe|used_in_sections:
482hx|  - background
483nx|  - methodology
484hq|local_file: "literature/pdfs/vaswani2017.pdf"
485cz|key_findings:
486iq|  - Self-attention can replace recurrence for sequence modeling
487te|  - Multi-head attention enables capturing different representation subspaces
488mm|  - Transformer achieves SOTA on WMT 2014 EN-DE and EN-FR
489th|methodology_notes: Encoder-decoder architecture, multi-head attention, positional encoding. Training: 8 P100 GPUs, 3.5 days.
490oa|personal_notes: The core reference for our transformer variant. Key equations: scaled dot-product attention (Eq 1), multi-head attention (Eq 2).
491bt|---
492nd|
493gc|### Summary
494th|
495oy|[1–3 sentence synthesis of the paper's contribution and relevance to your research.]
496xc|```
497th|
498hd|**Rules for citation keys:** `authorYYYYkeyword` — first author last name, year, first meaningful word from title. Lowercase. Example: `vaswani2017attention`.
499th|
500vf|---
