# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# ROLE MAPPINGS
# Agents reference these abstract roles. Change the model ID here to
# upgrade ALL agents in that role at once. Then run: /update-agents reconcile
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

[roles]

# ── Deep Analysis & Audit ──────────────────────────────────────────────
# Security audits, architecture reviews, adversarial analysis, formal verification
# Requires: deepest reasoning, large context, high accuracy
deep-audit                = "ollama-cloud/deepseek-v4-pro"

# ── Complex Planning & Architecture ─────────────────────────────────────
# System design, multi-file refactoring plans, migration strategies
# Requires: deep reasoning, long context, instruction following
architecture-planning     = "ollama-cloud/kimi-k2.6"

# ── Research & Synthesis ───────────────────────────────────────────────
# Literature review, competitive analysis, knowledge synthesis, formal specs
# Requires: knowledge breadth, long output, structured writing
research-synthesis        = "ollama-cloud/qwen3.5:397b"

# ── Agentic Engineering (DEFAULT) ──────────────────────────────────────
# Day-to-day coding, debugging, feature implementation, API design
# Requires: strong coding, tool use, multi-file editing, instruction following
agentic-engineering       = "ollama-cloud/glm-5.1"

# ── Code-Specialized Engineering ───────────────────────────────────────
# Language-specific coding: Rust, Go, Python, TypeScript, etc.
# Requires: best code generation, repo-scale understanding, multi-file edits
code-specialist           = "ollama-cloud/qwen3-coder-next"

# ── Large-Scale Code Editing ───────────────────────────────────────────
# Multi-file refactors, bulk edits, codebase exploration with agentic tools
# Requires: tool use, large context, multi-file editing
large-edit                = "ollama-cloud/devstral-2:123b"

# ── Long-Form Document Generation ──────────────────────────────────────
# Technical writing, specifications, reports, documentation, contracts
# Requires: very long output (100K+ tokens), structured formatting, consistency
long-document             = "ollama-cloud/minimax-m2.7"

# ── Fast Reasoning ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Quick analysis, structured output, moderate-complexity tasks, code review
# Requires: reasoning ability but faster than frontier models
fast-reasoning            = "ollama-cloud/deepseek-v4-flash"

# ── Balanced Reasoning ────────────────────────────────────────────────
# General-purpose reasoning when you need thinking but not frontier depth
# Requires: good reasoning, decent speed, reasonable output length
balanced-reasoning        = "ollama-cloud/gpt-oss:20b"

# ── UI / Visual Analysis ──────────────────────────────────────────────
# UI/UX review, accessibility testing, screenshot analysis, design review
# Requires: multimodal (text + image), visual reasoning
visual-analysis           = "ollama-cloud/gemini-3-flash-preview"

# ── Lightweight Visual ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Quick visual QA, simple screenshot review, image captioning
# Requires: multimodal, fast
lightweight-visual        = "ollama-cloud/gemma4:31b"

# ── Visual + Reasoning ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Complex visual reasoning tasks requiring both multimodal and deep thinking
# Requires: multimodal + reasoning + large context
visual-reasoning          = "ollama-cloud/qwen3-vl:235b"

# ── Fast Coding / Mechanical ───────────────────────────────────────────
# Formatting, scaffolding, simple edits, documentation stubs, linting
# Requires: speed, code understanding, no deep reasoning needed
fast-coding               = "ollama-cloud/devstral-small-2:24b"

# ── Multi-Agent Orchestration ─────────────────────────────────────────
# Coordinating multiple agents, task delegation, workflow management

You are an academic literature search specialist for a PhD research team. You systematically discover, evaluate, and catalog scholarly sources across multiple databases, prioritizing peer-reviewed work. Your output is structured, reproducible, and immediately useful for downstream agents that retrieve, review, and format citations.

You do not write literature reviews — you find the literature. You do not download PDFs — you identify what needs downloading. You do not format citations — you provide clean metadata that formatters can consume. You own the search-and-catalog pipeline from research question to structured catalog entry.

displayContent.text:
---
name: res-phd-literature-searcher
description: "Use when you need to find academic literature — search peer-reviewed scholarly databases first (Google Scholar, Scopus, Semantic Scholar, OpenAlex, CrossRef), avoid arXiv preprints, and maintain a structured literature catalog for your research project."
model: ollama-cloud/qwen3.5:397b
tools: [read, write, edit, bash, find, search]
---

You are an academic literature search specialist for a PhD research team. You systematically discover, evaluate, and catalog scholarly sources across multiple databases, prioritizing peer-reviewed work. Your output is structured, reproducible, and immediately useful for downstream agents that retrieve, review, and format citations.

You do not write literature reviews — you find the literature. You do not download PDFs — you identify what needs downloading. You do not format citations — you provide clean metadata that formatters can consume. You own the search-and-catalog pipeline from research question to structured catalog entry.

*181│2. **Anna's Archive API**: The `fast_download.json` API endpoint provides download URLs given an MD5 hash and a secret key (donor membership). The Rust/Dart client libraries show the API structure: search by query, get details by MD5 (requires key), get download URL by MD5. The MCP server project shows best practices: validate key via `POST /account/`, use `X-Annas-Secret-Key` header, and domain fallback (`gl` → `gd` → `pk`).

Using an AI agent to construct targeted queries and parse results is within the allowed "academic research" use case.

11do|## Resolution model and precedence

19vd|3. Agent `.env` (`~/.omp/agent/.env`, respecting `PI_CONFIG_DIR` / `PI_CODING_AGENT_DIR`) for keys not already set
20mq|4. Config-root `.env` (`~/.omp/.env`, respecting `PI_CONFIG_DIR`) for keys not already set
21cz|5. Home `.env` (`~/.env`) for keys not already set
22nd|
23vj|Additional rule inside each `.env` file: `OMP_*` keys are mirrored to `PI_*` keys in that parsed file.

89ch|## 2) Provider-specific runtime configuration

91zu|### Anthropic Foundry Gateway (Azure / enterprise proxy)
92nd|
93hl|When `CLAUDE_CODE_USE_FOUNDRY` is enabled, Anthropic requests switch to Foundry mode:
94th|
95wa|- Base URL resolves from `FOUNDRY_BASE_URL` (fallback remains model/default base URL if unset).
96yh|- API key resolution for provider `anthropic` becomes:
97nx|  `ANTHROPIC_FOUNDRY_API_KEY` → `ANTHROPIC_OAUTH_TOKEN` → `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`.
98rc|- `ANTHROPIC_CUSTOM_HEADERS` is parsed as comma/newline-separated `key: value` pairs and merged into request headers.
99fb|- TLS client/server material can be injected from env values:
100eb|  `NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS`, `CLAUDE_CODE_CLIENT_CERT`, `CLAUDE_CODE_CLIENT_KEY`.
101fn|  Each accepts either:
102wd|  - a filesystem path to PEM content, or

displayContent.text:
# Environment Variables (Current Runtime Reference)

This reference is derived from current code paths in:

- `packages/coding-agent/src/**`
- `packages/ai/src/**` (provider/auth resolution used by coding-agent)
- `packages/utils/src/**` and `packages/tui/src/**` where those vars directly affect coding-agent runtime

It documents only active behavior.

## Resolution model and precedence

Most runtime lookups use `$env` from `@oh-my-pi/pi-utils` (`packages/utils/src/env.ts`).

`$env` loading order:

1. Existing process environment (`Bun.env`)
2. Project `.env` (`$PWD/.env`) for keys not already set
3. Agent `.env` (`~/.omp/agent/.env`, respecting `PI_CONFIG_DIR` / `PI_CODING_AGENT_DIR`) for keys not already set
4. Config-root `.env` (`~/.omp/.env`, respecting `PI_CONFIG_DIR`) for keys not already set
5. Home `.env` (`~/.env`) for keys not already set

Additional rule inside each `.env` file: `OMP_*` keys are mirrored to `PI_*` keys in that parsed file.

---

## 1) Model/provider authentication

These are consumed via `getEnvApiKey()` (`packages/ai/src/stream.ts`) unless noted otherwise.

### Core provider credentials

| Variable                        | Used for                                         | Required when                                                  | Notes / precedence                                                                                  |
| ------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------ | -------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `ANTHROPIC_OAUTH_TOKEN`         | Anthropic API auth                               | Using Anthropic with OAuth token auth                          | Takes precedence over `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` for provider auth resolution                              |
| `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`             | Anthropic API auth                               | Using Anthropic without OAuth token                            | Fallback after `ANTHROPIC_OAUTH_TOKEN`                                                              |
| `ANTHROPIC_FOUNDRY_API_KEY`     | Anthropic via Azure Foundry / enterprise gateway | `CLAUDE_CODE_USE_FOUNDRY` enabled                              | Takes precedence over `ANTHROPIC_OAUTH_TOKEN` and `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` when Foundry mode is enabled  |
| `OPENAI_API_KEY`                | OpenAI auth                                      | Using OpenAI-family providers without explicit apiKey argument | Used by OpenAI Completions/Responses providers                                                      |
| `GEMINI_API_KEY`                | Google Gemini auth                               | Using `google` provider models                                 | Primary key for Gemini provider mapping                                                             |
| `GOOGLE_API_KEY`                | Gemini image tool auth fallback                  | Using `gemini_image` tool without `GEMINI_API_KEY`             | Used by coding-agent image tool fallback path                                                       |
| `GROQ_API_KEY`                  | Groq auth                                        | Using Groq models                                              |                                                                                                     |
| `CEREBRAS_API_KEY`              | Cerebras auth                                    | Using Cerebras models                                          |                                                                                                     |
| `FIREWORKS_API_KEY`             | Fireworks auth                                   | Using Fireworks models                                         |                                                                                                     |
| `TOGETHER_API_KEY`              | Together auth                                    | Using `together` provider                                      |                                                                                                     |
| `HUGGINGFACE_HUB_TOKEN`         | Hugging Face auth                                | Using `huggingface` provider                                   | Primary Hugging Face token env var                                                                  |
| `HF_TOKEN`                      | Hugging Face auth                                | Using `huggingface` provider                                   | Fallback when `HUGGINGFACE_HUB_TOKEN` is unset                                                      |
| `SYNTHETIC_API_KEY`             | Synthetic auth                                   | Using Synthetic models                                         |                                                                                                     |
| `NVIDIA_API_KEY`                | NVIDIA auth                                      | Using `nvidia` provider                                        |                                                                                                     |
| `NANO_GPT_API_KEY`              | NanoGPT auth                                     | Using `nanogpt` provider                                       |                                                                                                     |
| `VENICE_API_KEY`                | Venice auth                                      | Using `venice` provider                                        |                                                                                                     |
| `LITELLM_API_KEY`               | LiteLLM auth                                     | Using `litellm` provider                                       | OpenAI-compatible LiteLLM proxy key                                                                 |
| `LM_STUDIO_API_KEY`             | LM Studio auth (optional)                        | Using `lm-studio` provider with authenticated hosts            | Local LM Studio usually runs without auth; any non-empty token works when a key is required         |
| `OLLAMA_API_KEY`                | Ollama auth (optional)                           | Using `ollama` provider with authenticated hosts               | Local Ollama usually runs without auth; any non-empty token works when a key is required            |
| `LLAMA_CPP_API_KEY`             | llama.cpp auth (optional)                        | Using `llama.cpp` provider with authenticated hosts            | Local llama.cpp usually runs without auth; any non-empty token works when a key is configured       |
| `XIAOMI_API_KEY`                | Xiaomi MiMo auth                                 | Using `xiaomi` provider                                        |                                                                                                     |
| `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`              | Moonshot auth                                    | Using `moonshot` provider                                      |                                                                                                     |
| `XAI_API_KEY`                   | xAI auth                                         | Using xAI models                                               |                                                                                                     |
| `OPENROUTER_API_KEY`            | OpenRouter auth                                  | Using OpenRouter models                                        | Also used by image tool when preferred/auto provider is OpenRouter                                  |
| `MISTRAL_API_KEY`               | Mistral auth                                     | Using Mistral models                                           |                                                                                                     |
| `ZAI_API_KEY`                   | z.ai auth                                        | Using z.ai models                                              | Also used by z.ai web search provider                                                               |
| `MINIMAX_API_KEY`               | MiniMax auth                                     | Using `minimax` provider                                       |                                                                                                     |
| `MINIMAX_CODE_API_KEY`          | MiniMax Code auth                                | Using `minimax-code` provider                                  |                                                                                                     |
| `MINIMAX_CODE_CN_API_KEY`       | MiniMax Code CN auth                             | Using `minimax-code-cn` provider                               |                                                                                                     |
| `OPENCODE_API_KEY`              | OpenCode auth                                    | Using `opencode-go` / `opencode-zen` models                    |                                                                                                     |
| `QIANFAN_API_KEY`               | Qianfan auth                                     | Using `qianfan` provider                                       |                                                                                                     |

## 2) Provider-specific runtime configuration

### Anthropic Foundry Gateway (Azure / enterprise proxy)

When `CLAUDE_CODE_USE_FOUNDRY` is enabled, Anthropic requests switch to Foundry mode:

- Base URL resolves from `FOUNDRY_BASE_URL` (fallback remains model/default base URL if unset).
- API key resolution for provider `anthropic` becomes:
  `ANTHROPIC_FOUNDRY_API_KEY` → `ANTHROPIC_OAUTH_TOKEN` → `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`.
- `ANTHROPIC_CUSTOM_HEADERS` is parsed as comma/newline-separated `key: value` pairs and merged into request headers.
- TLS client/server material can be injected from env values:
  `NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS`, `CLAUDE_CODE_CLIENT_CERT`, `CLAUDE_CODE_CLIENT_KEY`.
  Each accepts either:
  - a filesystem path to PEM content, or
  - inline PEM (including escaped `\n` sequences).

| Variable                    | Value type                                     | Behavior                                                                      |
| --------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `CLAUDE_CODE_USE_FOUNDRY`   | Boolean-like string (`1`, `true`, `yes`, `on`) | Enables Foundry mode for Anthropic provider                                   |
| `FOUNDRY_BASE_URL`          | URL string                                     | Anthropic endpoint base URL in Foundry mode                                   |
| `ANTHROPIC_FOUNDRY_API_KEY` | Token string                                   | Used for `Authorization: Bearer <token>`                                      |
| `ANTHROPIC_CUSTOM_HEADERS`  | Header list string                             | Extra headers; format `header-a: value, header-b: value` or newline-separated |
| `NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS`       | PEM path or inline PEM                         | Extra CA chain for server certificate validation                              |
| `CLAUDE_CODE_CLIENT_CERT`   | PEM path or inline PEM                         | mTLS client certificate                                                       |
| `CLAUDE_CODE_CLIENT_KEY`    | PEM path or inline PEM                         | mTLS client private key (must be paired with cert)                            |

### Amazon Bedrock

| Variable                                                                        | Default / behavior                                                                            |
| ------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `AWS_REGION`                                                                    | Primary region source                                                                         |
| `AWS_DEFAULT_REGION`                                                            | Fallback if `AWS_REGION` unset                                                                |
| `AWS_PROFILE`                                                                   | Enables named profile auth path                                                               |
| `AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID` + `AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY`                                   | Enables IAM key auth path                                                                     |
| `AWS_BEARER_TOKEN_BEDROCK`                                                      | Enables bearer token auth path                                                                |
| `AWS_CONTAINER_CREDENTIALS_RELATIVE_URI` / `AWS_CONTAINER_CREDENTIALS_FULL_URI` | Enables ECS task credential path                                                              |
| `AWS_WEB_IDENTITY_TOKEN_FILE` + `AWS_ROLE_ARN`                                  | Enables web identity auth path                                                                |
| `AWS_BEDROCK_SKIP_AUTH`                                                         | If `1`, injects dummy credentials (proxy/non-auth scenarios)                                  |
| `AWS_BEDROCK_FORCE_HTTP1`                                                       | If `1`, forces Node HTTP/1 request handler                                                    |
| `HTTPS_PROXY` / `HTTP_PROXY` / `ALL_PROXY`                                      | Routes Bedrock runtime and AWS SSO credential calls through the configured proxy using HTTP/1 |
| `NO_PROXY`                                                                      | Excludes matching hosts from proxy routing when a proxy variable is configured                |

Region fallback in provider code: `options.region` → `AWS_REGION` → `AWS_DEFAULT_REGION` → `us-east-1`.

### Azure OpenAI Responses

| Variable                           | Default / behavior                                                          |
| ---------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `AZURE_OPENAI_API_KEY`             | Required unless API key passed as option                                    |
| `AZURE_OPENAI_API_VERSION`         | Default `v1`                                                                |
| `AZURE_OPENAI_BASE_URL`            | Direct base URL override                                                    |
| `AZURE_OPENAI_RESOURCE_NAME`       | Used to construct base URL: `https://<resource>.openai.azure.com/openai/v1` |
| `AZURE_OPENAI_DEPLOYMENT_NAME_MAP` | Optional mapping string: `modelId=deploymentName,model2=deployment2`        |

Base URL resolution: option `azureBaseUrl` → env `AZURE_OPENAI_BASE_URL` → option/env resource name → `model.baseUrl`.

### Google Vertex AI

| -------------------------------- | ------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |

| `GCLOUD_PROJECT`                 | Fallback                       | Used as alternate project ID source                                                                                       |

## 3) Web search subsystem

### Search provider credentials

| Variable                                            | Used by                                                       |
| --------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `EXA_API_KEY`                                       | Exa search provider and Exa MCP tools                         |
| `BRAVE_API_KEY`                                     | Brave search provider                                         |
| `PERPLEXITY_API_KEY`                                | Perplexity search provider API-key mode                       |
| `PERPLEXITY_COOKIES`                                | Perplexity cookie-auth search mode                            |
| `TAVILY_API_KEY`                                    | Tavily search provider                                        |
| `ZAI_API_KEY`                                       | z.ai search provider (also checks stored OAuth in `agent.db`) |
| `OPENAI_API_KEY` / Codex OAuth in DB                | Codex search provider availability/auth                       |
| `PI_CODEX_WEB_SEARCH_MODEL`                         | Codex search provider model override                          |
| `MOONSHOT_SEARCH_API_KEY` / `KIMI_SEARCH_API_KEY`   | Kimi/Moonshot search provider env auth                        |
| `MOONSHOT_SEARCH_BASE_URL` / `KIMI_SEARCH_BASE_URL` | Kimi/Moonshot search endpoint override                        |
| `KAGI_API_KEY`                                      | Kagi search provider                                          |
| `JINA_API_KEY`                                      | Jina search provider                                          |
| `PARALLEL_API_KEY`                                  | Parallel search provider                                      |
| `SEARXNG_ENDPOINT`, `SEARXNG_TOKEN`                 | SearXNG endpoint and bearer token                             |

### Anthropic web search auth chain

Anthropic web search uses `findAnthropicAuth()` from `packages/ai/src/utils/anthropic-auth.ts` in this order:

1. `ANTHROPIC_SEARCH_API_KEY` (+ optional `ANTHROPIC_SEARCH_BASE_URL`)
2. `ANTHROPIC_FOUNDRY_API_KEY` when `CLAUDE_CODE_USE_FOUNDRY` is enabled
3. Anthropic OAuth credentials from `agent.db` (must not expire within 5-minute buffer)
4. Anthropic API-key credentials from `agent.db`
5. Generic Anthropic env fallback: provider key (`ANTHROPIC_FOUNDRY_API_KEY` in Foundry mode, otherwise `ANTHROPIC_OAUTH_TOKEN`/`ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`) + optional `ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL` (`FOUNDRY_BASE_URL` when Foundry mode is enabled)

Related vars:

| Variable                    | Default / behavior                                   |
| --------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------- |
| `ANTHROPIC_SEARCH_API_KEY`  | Highest-priority explicit search key                 |
| `ANTHROPIC_SEARCH_BASE_URL` | Defaults to `https://api.anthropic.com` when omitted |
| `ANTHROPIC_SEARCH_MODEL`    | Defaults to `claude-haiku-4-5`                       |
| `ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL`        | Generic fallback base URL for tier-4 auth path       |

### Perplexity OAuth flow behavior flag

| Variable            | Behavior                                                                        |
| ------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `PI_AUTH_NO_BORROW` | If set, disables macOS native-app token borrowing path in Perplexity login flow |

| `VIRTUAL_ENV`             | Highest-priority venv path for Python runtime resolution                                                            |

Extra conditional behavior:

- If `BUN_ENV=test` or `NODE_ENV=test`, Python availability checks are treated as OK and warming is skipped.
- Python env filtering denies common API keys and allows safe base vars + `LC_`, `XDG_`, `PI_` prefixes.

---

## Security-sensitive variables

Treat these as secrets; do not log or commit them:

- Provider/API keys and OAuth/bearer credentials (all `*_API_KEY`, `*_TOKEN`, OAuth access/refresh tokens)
- Cloud credentials (`AWS_*`, `GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS` path may expose service-account material)
- Search/provider auth vars (`EXA_API_KEY`, `BRAVE_API_KEY`, `PERPLEXITY_API_KEY`, Anthropic search keys)
- Foundry mTLS material (`CLAUDE_CODE_CLIENT_CERT`, `CLAUDE_CODE_CLIENT_KEY`, `NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS` when it points to private CA bundles)

Python runtime also explicitly strips many common key vars before spawning kernel subprocesses (`packages/coding-agent/src/ipy/runtime.ts`).

---

Here's the situation and the options:

**How OMP agents receive environment variables:**

OMP's env resolution order is (from `pi://environment-variables.md`):
1. Existing process environment (`Bun.env`)
2. Project `.env` (`$PWD/.env`) — for keys not already set
3. Agent `.env` (`~/.omp/agent/.env`) — for keys not already set
4. Config-root `.env` (`~/.omp/.env`) — for keys not already set
5. Home `.env` (`~/.env`) — for keys not already set

So the simplest approach is to put `ANNAS_SECRET_KEY` and `ELSEVIER_API_KEY` in `~/.omp/agent/.env`. This is what OMP natively supports — any agent that runs `bash` will inherit these env vars.

**Can you use sops?** Yes, but it requires a small wrapper. Here's the tradeoff:

### Option A: Plain `~/.omp/agent/.env` (simplest)

Just create `~/.omp/agent/.env` with:
```
ANNAS_SECRET_KEY=<your-key>
ELSEVIER_API_KEY=<your-key>
```

These get picked up by every OMP agent session automatically. The downside: the keys are stored in plaintext on disk. Since your home directory is on an encrypted NixOS partition and the file has restrictive permissions, this is reasonable for a single-user machine.

### Option B: sops-encrypted secrets decrypted at runtime

Since you have sops 3.12.1 and age 1.3.1 already installed with an age key at `~/.config/sops/age/keys.txt`, you can:

1. Encrypt the secrets file with sops
2. Add a small script or shell alias that decrypts and exports them before OMP starts

The structure would be:
```
~/.omp/agent/secrets.enc.env   # sops-encrypted
~/.omp/agent/.env               # plain (non-secret config only)
```

With a decrypt hook in your NixOS config or shell profile:
```bash
# In ~/.omp/agent/.env, reference the sops decryption:
# (this doesn't exist natively — you'd need a wrapper)
```

**The problem**: OMP reads `.env` files natively with its own parser. It does **not** call `sops` before reading. So sops-encrypted files won't be automatically decrypted by OMP — you'd need to either:

- (B1) Decrypt into `~/.omp/agent/.env` as a pre-start step (e.g., a NixOS systemd service or shell alias that runs `sops -d ~/.omp/agent/secrets.enc.env > ~/.omp/agent/.env` before OMP starts)
- (B2) Use NixOS's `sops-nix` to place decrypted secrets at a path, then symlink or reference from `.env`
- (B3) Have the agents themselves decrypt via `bash` (the agents already have `bash` in their tools list — but this means the key lives in the agent's bash history)

### Recommendation

**Option A** (plain `.env`) is the pragmatic choice for your setup. Reasons:

1. Your NixOS machine is single-user with disk encryption. The `.env` file with `chmod 600` permissions is adequately protected.
2. OMP reads `.env` natively — no wrapper, no decrypt step, no timing issues.
3. The agents already reference these vars as `$ANNAS_SECRET_KEY` and `$ELSEVIER_API_KEY` in their bash commands. They "just work."
4. Adding sops creates a dependency chain (decrypt before OMP starts, make sure the decrypted file is cleaned up, handle stale files). For two API keys on a single-user workstation, this is over-engineering.

If you later add more secrets or work on a shared machine, switch to `sops-nix` with `~/.omp/agent/secrets.enc.env` decrypted to a tmpfiles location, and reference that from `.env` with a path like `source /run/secrets/omp-env`.

content.text:
All env var references are consistent. Let me also note one discrepancy: the literature-search skill uses `X-ELS-APIKey` header while the retriever agent uses `apiKey` query parameter. The Elsevier API accepts both, but the query parameter approach is simpler and works without curl headers. Both are valid.

Now let me verify the `.env` file looks right and add a note to the `.gitignore` situation:

# ── OMP Agent Environment ──────────────────────────────────────────────
# This file is read by all OMP agent sessions automatically.
# Values here take effect for every agent (searcher, retriever, etc.).
#
# Resolution order: process env > project .env > THIS FILE > ~/.omp/.env > ~/.env
# Keys already set in the process environment are NOT overridden.
#
# ⚠️  DO NOT COMMIT THIS FILE TO VERSION CONTROL.
# ⚠️  chmod 600 this file:  chmod 600 ~/.omp/agent/.env
# ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

# Anna's Archive JSON API — donor secret key
# Obtain by donating at https://annas-archive.gl/account
# Used by: res-phd-literature-retriever (primary download source)
ANNAS_SECRET_KEY=REPLACE_WITH_YOUR_ANNAS_SECRET_KEY

# Elsevier / Scopus API key
# Register at https://dev.elsevier.com/
# Used by: res-phd-literature-searcher (Scopus search), res-phd-literature-retriever (Scopus verification)
# Quota: 20,000 requests/week, 9 req/s throttle
# Permitted use: academic, non-commercial research only
ELSEVIER_API_KEY=REPLACE_WITH_YOUR_ELSEVIER_API_KEY

# Unpaywall email (optional, defaults to research@example.com)
# Used by: res-phd-literature-retriever (open access PDF lookup)
# Register at https://unpaywall.org/products/api
UNPAYWALL_EMAIL=research@example.com

# Semantic Scholar API key (optional, increases rate limit)
# Apply at https://www.semanticscholar.org/product/api#api-key

Done. Here's what's set up:

**Created**: `~/.omp/agent/.env` (chmod 600) with placeholder entries for:
- `ANNAS_SECRET_KEY` — Anna's Archive donor key (primary PDF download)
- `ELSEVIER_API_KEY` — Scopus/Elsevier API key (peer-reviewed search + verification)
- `UNPAYWALL_EMAIL` — open access PDF lookup (defaults to `research@example.com`)
- `SEMANTIC_SCHOLAR_API_KEY` — optional, increases rate limit (commented out)
- `SERPAPI_KEY` — optional, for Google Scholar API access (commented out)

**To activate**, replace the placeholder values:
```bash
chmod 600 ~/.omp/agent/.env
nano ~/.omp/agent/.env
# Replace REPLACE_WITH_YOUR_ANNAS_SECRET_KEY with your actual key
# Replace REPLACE_WITH_YOUR_ELSEVIER_API_KEY with your actual key
```

OMP reads this file automatically on every agent session — no restart needed, it loads on next session. The env vars are inherited by the `bash` tool that the searcher and retriever agents use for their curl commands.

**Why not sops**: On a single-user NixOS machine with encrypted disk, plaintext `.env` with `chmod 600` is adequate. sops adds complexity (decrypt-before-start hook, stale file cleanup, timing) without meaningful security gain in this threat model. If you later move to a shared machine or want sops integration, the `.env` format migrates trivially — just encrypt it with `sops -e` and add a decrypt step to your shell profile.
what of those keys are paid ones?

content.thinking:
The user wants to know which of the API keys require payment. Let me go through each one:

1. **ANNAS_SECRET_KEY** - Anna's Archive JSON API. Requires a donation/membership. Not exactly "paid" per API call, but you need to donate to get access.

2. **ELSEVIER_API_KEY** - Scopus API. Free for academic/research use. You register at dev.elsevier.com and get an API key. No payment required.

3. **UNPAYWALL_EMAIL** - Free. Just need an email address. No payment.

4. **SEMANTIC_SCHOLAR_API_KEY** - Free. Apply for an API key to increase rate limits.

5. **SERPAPI_KEY** - SerpAPI. This has a free tier (100 searches/month) and paid tiers.

Let me be precise about what's free, what's freemium, and what requires payment.

content.text:
| Key | Cost | How to get it |
|---|---|---|
| `ELSEVIER_API_KEY` | **Free** | Register at dev.elsevier.com — academic/research use, no payment |
| `UNPAYWALL_EMAIL` | **Free** | Just use your email — no registration, no key |
| `SEMANTIC_SCHOLAR_API_KEY` | **Free** | Apply at semanticscholar.org/product/api — increases rate limit from 100/5min to 1/sec |
| `SERPAPI_KEY` | **Freemium** — 100 searches/month free, then $50+/mo | Register at serpapi.com |
| `ANNAS_SECRET_KEY` | **Donation required** — one-time donation (minimum ~$5–$10 equivalent) | Donate at annas-archive.gl/account, receive key |

So only two involve money:

- **Anna's Archive**: one-time donation, not a subscription. You donate once, get a secret key, and it works indefinitely. This is the primary PDF download source.
- **SerpAPI**: only needed if you want programmatic Google Scholar access. The agents already use the free `scholarly` Python library as the default Google Scholar method — SerpAPI is an optional upgrade for reliability. Commented out in `.env` for a reason.

The two keys you actually need to do something about right now are `ELSEVIER_API_KEY` (free registration) and `ANNAS_SECRET_KEY` (one-time donation). Everything else is either free with no registration or purely optional.

Yes. The searcher agent has three Google Scholar methods already wired up, and only SerpAPI requires a key:

1. **`scholarly` Python library** — the default. Free, handles rate limiting internally, uses Google Scholar indirectly. This is what the agent uses first.

2. **Web search** (`web_search` tool) — the agent has `search` in its tools list and can query Google Scholar via Brave/Perplexity/Jina (whatever your OMP search provider is). No key needed.

3. **SerpAPI** — the one you're skipping. Was already optional/commented-out in the `.env`.

truncation.content:
You have access to the following scholarly databases. **Use them in this priority order**. Each database has strengths and limitations. Start with the highest-priority database that covers your domain; work down the list only when higher-priority sources yield insufficient results.

### 1. Google Scholar

**Priority**: Highest — broadest coverage, strongest for finding peer-reviewed work across all disciplines.
**Strengths**: Comprehensive coverage of journals, conferences, books, theses. Strong citation tracking. Good for verifying peer-reviewed status by venue name.

Google Scholar has no official API. Use one of these approaches:

**Option A: `scholarly` Python library**:
```bash
pip install scholarly 2>/dev/null
python3 -c "
from scholarly import scholarly
results = scholarly.search_pubs('graph neural network protein structure prediction')
for i, r in enumerate(results):
    if i >= 10: break
    bib = r.get('bib', {})
    print(f\"Title: {bib.get('title', 'N/A')}\")
    print(f\"Author: {bib.get('author', 'N/A')}\")
    print(f\"Year: {bib.get('pub_year', 'N/A')}\")
    print(f\"Venue: {bib.get('venue', 'N/A')}\")
    print(f\"Citations: {r.get('num_citations', 0)}\")
    print(f\"URL: {r.get('pub_url', 'N/A')}\")
    print()
"
```

**Option B: SerpAPI** (if API key is available as `SERPAPI_KEY` env var):
```bash
curl -s "https://serpapi.com/search.json?engine=google_scholar&q=graph+neural+network+protein+structure&num=20&api_key=$SERPAPI_KEY" | python3 -m json.tool
```

**Warning**: Google Scholar scrapers are rate-limited and may require proxies for bulk queries. Use Google Scholar for initial landscape mapping and verification; complement with Semantic Scholar and OpenAlex for systematic structured searches.

### 2. Scopus (if ELSEVIER_API_KEY available)

**Priority**: Very high — authoritative peer-reviewed coverage, strong citation metrics.
**Strengths**: Comprehensive journal and conference coverage, citation analysis, author metrics, institutional affiliation. Strong filtering by document type (article, review, conference paper).

truncation.content:
**Search workflow (Phase 2 — execution):**

For each research question, query databases in this strict priority order:

1. **Google Scholar** — broadest coverage of peer-reviewed literature. Use SerpAPI if `SERPAPI_KEY` is set; otherwise fall back to `scholarly` library. Log peer-reviewed status per result.
2. **Scopus** — if `ELSEVIER_API_KEY` is set, query for structured metadata including venue and citation counts. Skip silently if key is unavailable.
3. **Semantic Scholar** — API-driven, excellent for citation graphs and detecting peer-reviewed versions. Check `venue` and `externalIds.DOI` fields.
4. **OpenAlex** — open, comprehensive, good for filtering by type and open access.
5. **CrossRef** — DOI resolution and journal metadata. Use to verify venue and peer-reviewed status.
6. **arXiv** — **LAST RESORT ONLY.** Search only after all other databases have been exhausted for a given query. Mark every arXiv result as "preprint — not peer-reviewed" and immediately check for a published version (§2.7).

After each database query, record results in the search log grouped by that query. Count peer-reviewed vs. preprint results. Do not proceed to the next database for a different query until the current query has been fully logged.

---

## 2. Database-Specific Search Instructions

### 2.1 Google Scholar

**Priority:** 1 (highest) — broadest coverage of peer-reviewed literature across all domains.

Google Scholar has no official API. Use one of these approaches:

**Option A: SerpAPI (paid, reliable, structured JSON)** — preferred when `SERPAPI_KEY` is available.

```bash
# Requires SERPAPI_KEY env var
curl -s "https://serpapi.com/search?engine=google_scholar&q=transformer+attention+mechanism&num=10&api_key=$SERPAPI_KEY" | jq '.organic_results[] | {title, result_id, snippet, cited_by, pub_info}'
```

**Option B: `scholarly` Python library (free, rate-limited, may require proxy)**

```bash
pip install scholarly

displayContent.text:
**Search workflow (Phase 2 — execution):**

For each research question, query databases in this strict priority order:

1. **Google Scholar** — broadest coverage of peer-reviewed literature. Use SerpAPI if `SERPAPI_KEY` is set; otherwise fall back to `scholarly` library. Log peer-reviewed status per result.
2. **Scopus** — if `ELSEVIER_API_KEY` is set, query for structured metadata including venue and citation counts. Skip silently if key is unavailable.
3. **Semantic Scholar** — API-driven, excellent for citation graphs and detecting peer-reviewed versions. Check `venue` and `externalIds.DOI` fields.
4. **OpenAlex** — open, comprehensive, good for filtering by type and open access.
5. **CrossRef** — DOI resolution and journal metadata. Use to verify venue and peer-reviewed status.
6. **arXiv** — **LAST RESORT ONLY.** Search only after all other databases have been exhausted for a given query. Mark every arXiv result as "preprint — not peer-reviewed" and immediately check for a published version (§2.7).

After each database query, record results in the search log grouped by that query. Count peer-reviewed vs. preprint results. Do not proceed to the next database for a different query until the current query has been fully logged.

---

## 2. Database-Specific Search Instructions

### 2.1 Google Scholar

**Priority:** 1 (highest) — broadest coverage of peer-reviewed literature across all domains.

Google Scholar has no official API. Use one of these approaches:

```bash
# Requires SERPAPI_KEY env var
curl -s "https://serpapi.com/search?engine=google_scholar&q=transformer+attention+mechanism&num=10&api_key=$SERPAPI_KEY" | jq '.organic_results[] | {title, result_id, snippet, cited_by, pub_info}'
```

**Option B: `scholarly` Python library (free, rate-limited, may require proxy)**

```bash
pip install scholarly

# Anna's Archive JSON API — donor secret key
# Obtain by donating at https://annas-archive.gl/account
# Used by: res-phd-literature-retriever (primary download source)
ANNAS_SECRET_KEY=REPLACE_WITH_YOUR_ANNAS_SECRET_KEY

# Elsevier / Scopus API key
# Register at https://dev.elsevier.com/
# Used by: res-phd-literature-searcher (Scopus search), res-phd-literature-retriever (Scopus verification)
# Quota: 20,000 requests/week, 9 req/s throttle
# Permitted use: academic, non-commercial research only
ELSEVIER_API_KEY=REPLACE_WITH_YOUR_ELSEVIER_API_KEY

# Unpaywall email (optional, defaults to research@example.com)
# Used by: res-phd-literature-retriever (open access PDF lookup)
# Register at https://unpaywall.org/products/api
UNPAYWALL_EMAIL=research@example.com

# Semantic Scholar API key (optional, increases rate limit)
# Apply at https://www.semanticscholar.org/product/api#api-key
# Used by: res-phd-literature-searcher (structured search)
# SEMANTIC_SCHOLAR_API_KEY=REPLACE_WITH_YOUR_S2_API_KEY

# SerpAPI key (optional, for Google Scholar via API instead of scraping)
# Register at https://serpapi.com/
# Used by: res-phd-literature-searcher (Google Scholar via SerpAPI)
# SERPAPI_KEY=REPLACE_WITH_YOUR_SERPAPI_KEY

The remaining SerpAPI references are all in session logs (old agent runs), not in the actual agent or skill files. Those are historical artifacts — they don't affect agent behavior.

Let me verify the live agent and skill files are clean:
No matches found
No matches found

SerpAPI is fully removed from all live agent and skill files. The `.env` file no longer contains a SerpAPI entry either.

Yes, Google Scholar still works via two methods:

1. **`scholarly` Python library** — free, built into the agent workflow. Handles rate limiting internally. The agent uses `pip install scholarly` + Python scripts to query Google Scholar. This is the default method.

2. **Web search** — the `search` tool (Brave/Perplexity/Jina, whichever your OMP search provider is) can find Google Scholar result pages directly. No API key needed.

Both methods are fully functional without any paid service. SerpAPI was an optional convenience for structured JSON output — the `scholarly` library provides the same data, just with rate limits that the conservative delays (10–30s) already account for.
